definition: Degrade or humble; to lower in rank, status, or esteem.
examples: He refused to abase himself by begging for the promotion.

definition: Reduce or diminish in intensity.
examples: The storm finally began to abate by nightfall.

definition: Abnormal, deviant; straying from the usual or expected.
examples: The scientist flagged the aberrant reading for a second review.

definition: Detest; regard with disgust.
examples: She abhors any form of cruelty toward animals.

definition: Give up or renounce, especially formally or under oath.
examples: The knight was forced to abjure his allegiance to the deposed king.

definition: Depart suddenly and secretly, typically to avoid capture.
examples: The treasurer absconded with the company's funds before the audit.

definition: Hold back or refrain, especially from something bad; decline to vote.
examples: Three council members chose to abstain from the controversial vote.

definition: Keen, quick, accurate insight or judgment.
examples: Her business acumen turned the failing startup into a success.

definition: Mildly scold; caution, advise, or remind to do something.
examples: The teacher admonished the students for their careless work.

definition: Make impure by adding inappropriate or inferior ingredients.
examples: The vendor was fined for adulterating the honey with cheap syrup.

definition: Opposing or harmful; unfavorable.
examples: The medication was withdrawn after several adverse reactions.

definition: Concerning the appreciation of beauty or good taste.
examples: The building's aesthetic appeal masked its poor construction.

definition: Warm, friendly, pleasant, and approachable.
examples: The affable host made every guest feel welcome.

definition: Make greater; exaggerate or inflate in power or reputation.
examples: The dictator built monuments to aggrandize his own legacy.

definition: Cheerful or speedy willingness; brisk eagerness.
examples: She accepted the challenge with surprising alacrity.

definition: Lessen; make easier to endure.
examples: The new bridge did much to alleviate the city's traffic.

definition: Distant physically or emotionally; reserved, indifferent.
examples: He remained aloof from the office gossip.

definition: Blend, merge, or unite into a single whole.
examples: The two banks amalgamated to form a single institution.

definition: Unclear or open to more than one interpretation.
examples: The contract's ambiguous wording led to a lawsuit.

definition: Uncertain; having mixed or contradictory feelings.
examples: She felt ambivalent about leaving her hometown for the job.

definition: Improve; make better or more bearable.
examples: The new policy was meant to ameliorate the workers' conditions.

definition: Something out of its correct historical time.
examples: The wristwatch in the ancient-Rome film was a glaring anachronism.

definition: Comparable; corresponding in some particular way.
examples: The heart is analogous to a pump that circulates fluid.

definition: A deviation from what is common or expected; inconsistency.
examples: The data point was an anomaly the team could not explain.

definition: Directly opposed; contrasting sharply.
examples: His reckless spending was antithetical to her frugal habits.

definition: Lack of feeling, interest, or concern; indifference.
examples: Voter apathy led to a record-low turnout.

definition: Of questionable authenticity; probably false.
examples: The story of his heroism turned out to be apocryphal.

definition: Praise or approval, especially formal approval.
examples: The proposal won the approbation of the entire board.

definition: A judge or umpire empowered to decide a disputed matter.
examples: The committee served as the final arbiter of the contest.

definition: Understood by only a few; obscure or secret.
examples: He delighted in the arcane rules of medieval heraldry.

definition: Characteristic of an earlier period; ancient or outdated.
examples: The law relied on archaic language no one still used.

definition: Very passionate, devoted, or enthusiastic.
examples: She was an ardent supporter of the reform movement.

definition: Very difficult and strenuous; hard to endure.
examples: The arduous climb exhausted even the fittest hikers.

definition: Using language clearly and fluently; to express distinctly.
examples: She gave an articulate defense of her thesis.

definition: Free of deceit or craft; natural and genuine.
examples: His artless honesty won over the wary jury.

definition: Practicing severe self-discipline and abstaining from pleasure.
examples: The monk led an ascetic life of prayer and fasting.

definition: Persevering, diligent, and constant in effort.
examples: Her assiduous note-taking paid off at exam time.

definition: Make milder; relieve, soothe, or pacify.
examples: A sincere apology did little to assuage her anger.

definition: Very bold or daring, sometimes recklessly so.
examples: The startup made an audacious bid for the market leader.

definition: Make larger; increase or add to.
examples: He took a second job to augment his income.

definition: Severe in manner or appearance; without luxury or ease.
examples: The room was austere, furnished with only a cot and a desk.

definition: Self-governing and independent.
examples: The region became autonomous after the treaty was signed.

definition: Insatiable greed; a miserly desire to hoard wealth.
examples: His avarice drove him to cheat his own family.

definition: Declare or affirm with confidence.
examples: The witness averred that she had seen the whole event.

definition: Lacking freshness or originality; predictable and cliché.
examples: The speech was full of banal remarks about teamwork.

definition: Contradict or misrepresent; give a false impression of.
examples: Her calm face belied the panic she felt inside.

definition: Strengthen or support; prop up.
examples: New evidence bolstered the prosecution's case.

definition: Far more showy or dramatic in speech than is warranted; pompous.
examples: The senator's bombastic speech impressed no one.

definition: A rude, ill-mannered, or insensitive person.
examples: Only a boor would ignore the host so pointedly.

definition: Relating to the pleasant aspects of the countryside; rural.
examples: The painting captured a bucolic scene of grazing sheep.

definition: Grow or flourish rapidly.
examples: The town's population burgeoned after the factory opened.

definition: A harsh, discordant mixture of sounds.
examples: A cacophony of car horns rose from the gridlocked street.

definition: Open, sincere, and honest.
examples: She was refreshingly candid about her own failures.

definition: Acting on impulse; erratic and unpredictable.
examples: The capricious weather ruined their outdoor plans.

definition: Criticize severely; punish in order to correct.
examples: The columnist castigated the mayor for ignoring the report.

definition: Something that causes or accelerates change.
examples: The protest became a catalyst for sweeping reform.

definition: Capable of burning; very critical or sarcastic.
examples: His caustic wit could deflate anyone's confidence.

definition: Strong disapproval or official reprimand.
examples: The senator faced censure for violating ethics rules.

definition: Trickery; deception by false or clever arguments.
examples: The deal collapsed once the lawyer's chicanery came to light.

definition: Cautious and prudent; careful of consequences.
examples: Investors grew circumspect after the market's sudden drop.

definition: Come together and unite; fuse into a whole.
examples: The scattered groups coalesced into a single movement.

definition: Clear, logical, and convincing.
examples: He made a cogent argument for reforming the tax code.

definition: Self-satisfied and smug; overly content and unmotivated.
examples: Years of easy profits made the company complacent.

definition: Eager to please; cheerfully compliant.
examples: The complaisant clerk agreed to every request without protest.

definition: Intended to appease or make peace.
examples: She struck a conciliatory tone to end the feud.

definition: Overlook, tolerate, or treat as acceptable.
examples: The school will not condone cheating of any kind.

definition: An expert of refined taste, especially in the fine arts.
examples: A true connoisseur, she could name the vintage by taste alone.

definition: Remorseful; feeling sorry for one's wrongdoing.
examples: He wrote a contrite letter apologizing for the damage.

definition: A confusing problem or riddle with no easy answer.
examples: Balancing growth and privacy is a modern conundrum.

definition: Twisted; extremely complicated and hard to follow.
examples: The plot was so convoluted that viewers lost track.

definition: Plentiful; present in large quantities.
examples: She took copious notes during the lecture.

definition: Support or confirm with evidence.
examples: A second witness corroborated her account of the accident.

definition: Contemptibly cowardly; lacking courage.
examples: His craven silence during the debate cost him the vote.

definition: Gullible; too ready to believe without evidence.
examples: The scam preyed on credulous first-time investors.

definition: Distrustful of others' motives; bitterly pessimistic.
examples: Years in politics left him cynical about promises.

definition: Discourage or dishearten; lessen the courage of.
examples: The steep learning curve did not daunt her.

definition: Degrade; lower in quality, value, or moral character.
examples: Flooding the market with copies debased the artist's work.

definition: Expose or disprove false or exaggerated claims.
examples: The study debunked the myth that the diet cured illness.

definition: Behaving with propriety and good taste; polite.
examples: The guests kept a decorous silence during the ceremony.

definition: Respectful submission; yielding to another's authority or opinion.
examples: Out of deference to her elders, she said nothing.

definition: Harmful or damaging, often gradually.
examples: Smoking has a deleterious effect on the lungs.

definition: Belittle; attack the reputation of.
examples: He tried to denigrate his rival's achievements.

definition: Mock or ridicule; laugh at contemptuously.
examples: Critics derided the film as shallow and clumsy.

definition: Imitative of others' work; not original.
examples: The album was dismissed as derivative and dull.

definition: Dry out thoroughly; dehydrate.
examples: The relentless sun desiccated the once-fertile plain.

definition: Lacking order or consistency; aimless and disconnected.
examples: He made only a desultory effort to clean the room.

definition: A bitter, abusive attack or criticism; a rant.
examples: Her review turned into a diatribe against modern art.

definition: A division into two opposed or contradictory groups.
examples: He rejected the dichotomy between work and play.

definition: Intended to instruct, often with a moral lesson.
examples: The novel is too didactic, lecturing at every turn.

definition: Lacking self-confidence; shy and modest.
examples: The diffident student rarely spoke up in class.

definition: Spread out widely; wordy and unfocused.
examples: His diffuse argument never reached a clear point.

definition: Stray from the main topic when speaking or writing.
examples: The lecturer digressed into a long personal story.

definition: Slow or late; tending to procrastinate or stall.
examples: Their dilatory tactics delayed the trial for months.

definition: One who dabbles in an art or field superficially.
examples: He was a dilettante who never finished a single painting.

definition: Free someone from a mistaken belief.
examples: Let me disabuse you of the idea that this will be easy.

definition: Having good judgment or insight; perceptive.
examples: A discerning editor caught the subtle error.

definition: Harsh and inharmonious; conflicting.
examples: The committee's discordant voices drowned out any plan.

definition: A difference or inconsistency.
examples: The auditor noticed a discrepancy in the accounts.

definition: Separate and distinct; existing as individual parts.
examples: The process breaks into five discrete stages.

definition: Insincere; not genuinely honest.
examples: It was disingenuous of him to feign surprise.

definition: Unbiased and impartial; free of personal stake.
examples: A disinterested referee was brought in to settle the dispute.

definition: Belittle or put down; speak of slightingly.
examples: She never disparaged her competitors in public.

definition: Fundamentally different; not comparable.
examples: The report drew on disparate sources of data.

definition: Calm and unbiased; free from emotion.
examples: The judge gave a dispassionate account of the facts.

definition: Scatter or spread widely; broadcast.
examples: The agency disseminated the warning across the region.

definition: Disagree or hold an opposing view, especially formally.
examples: Two justices dissented from the majority ruling.

definition: Conceal the truth or one's true feelings; put on a false appearance.
examples: He dissembled his disappointment behind a wide smile.

definition: A principle laid down by authority as unquestionably true.
examples: She refused to accept the party's dogma without question.

definition: Asleep or inactive; temporarily not functioning.
examples: The volcano had lain dormant for a century.

definition: Doubtful, questionable, or suspect.
examples: The claim rested on dubious evidence.

definition: Deceit; double-dealing to mislead others.
examples: His duplicity was revealed when both letters surfaced.

definition: Cheerful and full of energy; exuberant.
examples: The ebullient host greeted every guest with a hug.

definition: Peculiar or odd; deviating from convention.
examples: Her eccentric habits made her the talk of the office.

definition: Drawing from a broad, diverse range of sources.
examples: His eclectic taste in music ranged from opera to punk.

definition: The ability to produce a desired result.
examples: Trials confirmed the efficacy of the new vaccine.

definition: Shameless boldness; audacity.
examples: He had the effrontery to demand a raise after failing.

definition: Outstandingly or conspicuously bad; glaring.
examples: The report exposed an egregious abuse of power.

definition: Call forth or draw out a response.
examples: The question elicited an unexpected confession.

definition: Fluent and persuasive in speech; emotionally moving.
examples: Her eloquent plea swayed the entire jury.

definition: Decorate or adorn; enhance a story with invented details.
examples: He embellished the tale until it was barely true.

definition: Prominent, distinguished, and of high rank.
examples: An eminent scholar was invited to give the address.

definition: Based on observation or experiment rather than theory.
examples: The hypothesis still lacks empirical support.

definition: Imitate in an effort to equal or surpass.
examples: Young writers often emulate their favorite authors.

definition: Drain of energy or vitality; weaken.
examples: The relentless heat enervated the marchers.

definition: Produce or give rise to; cause to exist.
examples: The decision engendered fierce debate.

definition: A puzzle or mystery; a baffling person or thing.
examples: His sudden disappearance remains an enigma.

definition: Lasting only a short time; fleeting.
examples: The ephemeral beauty of the blossoms draws crowds each spring.

definition: Calmness and composure, especially under stress.
examples: She faced the crisis with remarkable equanimity.

definition: Use ambiguous language to avoid committing or to deceive.
examples: The spokesman equivocated when asked about the layoffs.

definition: Scholarly; possessing deep knowledge.
examples: The erudite professor could quote poetry in six languages.

definition: Deliberately avoid or abstain from.
examples: He eschews social media entirely.

definition: Understood by or intended for only a few.
examples: The lecture was too esoteric for a general audience.

definition: The characteristic spirit or moral values of a group.
examples: Hard work is central to the company's ethos.

definition: A speech or writing of high praise, especially for the dead.
examples: She delivered a moving eulogy at the funeral.

definition: A mild expression substituted for a harsh or blunt one.
examples: "Let go" is a common euphemism for being fired.

definition: Make a problem or bad situation worse.
examples: The tax hike only exacerbated the region's poverty.

definition: Clear from guilt or blame.
examples: New evidence exculpated the wrongly accused man.

definition: Requiring immediate attention; pressing or demanding.
examples: The exigent circumstances forced a quick decision.

definition: Clear from an accusation or responsibility.
examples: The inquiry exonerated the pilot of any fault.

definition: Convenient and practical, though sometimes improper.
examples: Cutting corners was expedient but ultimately costly.

definition: Irrelevant; not belonging or essential.
examples: The editor cut every extraneous detail.

definition: Joking, often inappropriately; not meant seriously.
examples: His facetious reply annoyed the interviewer.

definition: Based on a mistake in logic; deceptive or unsound.
examples: The ad rested on a fallacious appeal to fear.

definition: Excessively attentive to detail; hard to please.
examples: The fastidious editor caught every stray comma.

definition: Silly and pointless; foolishly self-satisfied.
examples: He made a fatuous remark that drew groans.

definition: Highly fertile; producing abundant growth or ideas.
examples: His fecund imagination produced a novel every season.

definition: Intensely passionate or enthusiastic.
examples: The crowd greeted the singer with fervid applause.

definition: Faithfulness and loyalty; accuracy of reproduction.
examples: The translation kept remarkable fidelity to the original.

definition: New and inexperienced; a beginner.
examples: The fledgling company had only three employees.

definition: Openly disregard a rule or convention with contempt.
examples: Drivers routinely flout the speed limit here.

definition: Incite or stir up trouble or unrest.
examples: Agitators tried to foment rebellion among the workers.

definition: Prevent or delay by taking action beforehand.
examples: The company cut prices to forestall a rival's entry.

definition: Happening by chance, typically a lucky one.
examples: Their meeting at the airport was entirely fortuitous.

definition: Economical and thrifty; not wasteful.
examples: Their frugal habits let them save for a house.

definition: Done secretly; stealthy and sly.
examples: He cast a furtive glance over his shoulder.

definition: Producing no useful result; pointless.
examples: Further argument was futile, so she left.

definition: Excessively talkative, especially about trivial matters.
examples: The garrulous cab driver talked the whole way downtown.

definition: Relevant and appropriate; on-topic.
examples: Her question was germane to the day's discussion.

definition: Fluent and easy in a way that suggests insincerity.
examples: His glib answers failed to reassure the investors.

definition: Sociable; fond of the company of others.
examples: The gregarious host knew everyone at the party.

definition: Clever deceit; cunning and craftiness.
examples: He won the negotiation through guile rather than strength.

definition: Overused to the point of being stale; unoriginal.
examples: The plot relied on hackneyed clichés.

definition: Calm, peaceful, and prosperous; idyllically happy.
examples: She looked back on the halcyon days of her youth.

definition: Unlucky; unfortunate.
examples: The hapless traveler missed every connecting flight.

definition: A long, intense, and often scolding verbal attack.
examples: The coach launched into a harangue about missed practices.

definition: Dominance or leadership, especially of one state over others.
examples: The empire's hegemony extended across three continents.

definition: Composed of different or diverse kinds of elements.
examples: The class was a heterogeneous mix of ages and backgrounds.

definition: Of the same kind; uniform throughout.
examples: The suburb was strikingly homogeneous in taste and income.

definition: One who attacks cherished beliefs or institutions.
examples: As an iconoclast, she delighted in overturning tradition.

definition: A peculiar habit or quirk specific to an individual.
examples: Chewing his pen was one of his many idiosyncrasies.

definition: Dishonorable; having base or low motives.
examples: He was ashamed of his ignoble desire for revenge.

definition: About to happen; impending.
examples: Dark clouds warned of an imminent storm.

definition: Unchangeable; not able to be altered.
examples: The laws of physics are, so far as we know, immutable.

definition: Unbiased and fair; not favoring one side.
examples: An impartial panel reviewed the complaints.

definition: Showing no emotion or physical feeling.
examples: Her impassive face gave nothing away.

definition: Having little or no money; poor.
examples: The impecunious student survived on bread and tea.

definition: Hinder or obstruct the progress of.
examples: Fallen trees impeded the rescue crews.

definition: Domineering and commanding; arrogantly overbearing.
examples: She dismissed the waiter with an imperious wave.

definition: Unable to be affected or penetrated.
examples: He seemed impervious to criticism.

definition: Acting on sudden impulse; hasty and rash.
examples: Her impetuous decision to quit surprised everyone.

definition: Unable to be appeased or calmed; relentless.
examples: He faced the implacable opposition of the board.

definition: Implied though not directly stated; unquestioning.
examples: There was an implicit warning in her tone.

definition: Unintentional; resulting from carelessness.
examples: An inadvertent error deleted the whole file.

definition: Just begun and not fully formed; rudimentary.
examples: The plan was still inchoate and full of gaps.

definition: In an early stage; just beginning to appear.
examples: Regulators watched the incipient industry closely.

definition: Out of place; not harmonious or fitting.
examples: The neon sign looked incongruous on the old stone church.

definition: Extreme poverty.
examples: The charity aids families living in indigence.

definition: Wanting to avoid activity or exertion; lazy.
examples: His indolent habits frustrated his ambitious partner.

definition: Inactive; having little or no power to move or react.
examples: The gas is inert and will not burn.

definition: Relentless; impossible to stop or persuade.
examples: The inexorable march of technology reshaped the industry.

definition: Innocent and unsuspecting; frank and candid.
examples: His ingenuous smile disarmed the skeptical crowd.

definition: Hostile; tending to obstruct or harm.
examples: The policy was inimical to free trade.

definition: Harmless; unlikely to offend or cause injury.
examples: What began as an innocuous joke spiraled into a feud.

definition: Lacking flavor, vigor, or interest; dull.
examples: Critics found the sequel insipid and forgettable.

definition: Narrow-minded; isolated and detached from broader views.
examples: The village had an insular distrust of outsiders.

definition: Refusing to compromise; stubbornly inflexible.
examples: The intransigent union rejected every proposal.

definition: Fearless and brave; enduring in the face of danger.
examples: The intrepid explorer crossed the frozen strait alone.

definition: Belonging to the essential nature of a thing.
examples: The painting has intrinsic as well as historical value.

definition: Flood or overwhelm.
examples: The office was inundated with complaints.

definition: Violent denunciation; abusive language.
examples: The debate degenerated into personal invective.

definition: Easily angered; quick-tempered.
examples: The irascible old man shouted at the delivery boy.

definition: Traveling from place to place, especially for work.
examples: He spent years as an itinerant musician.

definition: Discard or cast off, especially to lighten a load.
examples: The team jettisoned the plan when funding fell through.

definition: Showing good judgment; wise and sensible.
examples: A judicious use of resources kept the project alive.

definition: Place side by side for contrast or comparison.
examples: The exhibit juxtaposes ancient and modern art.

definition: Ignite or arouse; stir into being.
examples: The trip kindled her interest in geology.

definition: Using very few words; terse.
examples: His laconic reply was simply, "We'll see."

definition: Sluggish and slow; lacking energy or spirit.
examples: They spent a languid afternoon by the river.

definition: Existing but not yet visible or active; hidden.
examples: The training revealed a latent talent for design.

definition: Deserving praise; commendable.
examples: Her efforts to help were laudable, if unsuccessful.

definition: Abundant or extravagant; giving in great quantity.
examples: They threw a lavish party for the anniversary.

definition: Sluggish, drowsy, and lacking energy.
examples: The heat left everyone lethargic by noon.

definition: Lightness of mood; lack of seriousness.
examples: A moment of levity broke the tension in the room.

definition: Very talkative; wordy.
examples: The loquacious guide barely paused for breath.

definition: Clear and easy to understand; rational.
examples: She gave a lucid summary of the complex case.

definition: Looking or sounding sad and dismal.
examples: He recounted the tale in a lugubrious tone.

definition: Generous or forgiving, especially toward a rival.
examples: In victory she was magnanimous, praising her opponent.

definition: Easily shaped or influenced.
examples: Young recruits were seen as malleable and eager.

definition: An independent-minded rebel or dissenter.
examples: The maverick senator often broke with her party.

definition: Untruthful; habitually lying.
examples: The mendacious witness contradicted himself repeatedly.

definition: Subject to sudden, unpredictable changes of mood.
examples: His mercurial temper made him hard to work with.

definition: Showing great, precise attention to detail.
examples: She kept meticulous records of every transaction.

definition: Make less severe or painful; moderate.
examples: Sea walls were built to mitigate the flooding.

definition: Appease or soothe the anger of.
examples: He offered a refund to mollify the furious customer.

definition: Gloomy and sullen.
examples: He grew morose after the news of the loss.

definition: Common, ordinary, and everyday.
examples: Behind the mystery lay a mundane accounting error.

definition: Very generous; giving liberally.
examples: A munificent donor funded the entire wing.

definition: Short-sighted; lacking long-term thinking.
examples: The myopic policy ignored the coming crisis.

definition: The lowest point.
examples: The defeat marked the nadir of his career.

definition: Coming into existence; still developing.
examples: The nascent movement had few resources.

definition: Deny or refute; make ineffective.
examples: One careless remark negated hours of goodwill.

definition: A beginner or novice.
examples: As a neophyte, he made every rookie mistake.

definition: Fame for a disgraceful or unfavorable reason.
examples: The scandal brought him instant notoriety.

definition: A subtle difference in meaning, tone, or expression.
examples: A good translator captures every nuance.

definition: Stubborn; hardened against persuasion or feeling.
examples: He remained obdurate despite all the evidence.

definition: Excessively obedient or attentive; fawning.
examples: The obsequious waiter hovered anxiously by the table.

definition: Stubbornly refusing to change one's mind or course.
examples: The obstinate child refused to put on his coat.

definition: Prevent or make unnecessary.
examples: A clear contract would obviate future disputes.

definition: Meddlesome; overly eager to give unwanted advice.
examples: The officious clerk insisted on rechecking every form.

definition: Involving an oppressively heavy burden.
examples: The contract imposed onerous obligations on tenants.

definition: Not transparent; hard to understand or unclear.
examples: The instructions were so opaque no one could follow them.

definition: Harsh disgrace and public disapproval.
examples: The verdict brought opprobrium upon the whole firm.

definition: Conforming to established or traditional beliefs.
examples: She challenged the orthodox view of the disease.

definition: Showy; designed to impress or attract notice.
examples: The mansion's ostentatious decor drew stares.

definition: A model of excellence; a perfect example.
examples: She was held up as a paragon of professional integrity.

definition: Strongly devoted to a party or cause; biased.
examples: The bill split the chamber along partisan lines.

definition: Scarcity; smallness of number or quantity.
examples: There is a paucity of evidence for the claim.

definition: One who overemphasizes rules or shows off learning.
examples: Only a pedant would object to such a minor slip.

definition: A strong liking or inclination.
examples: She has a penchant for old jazz records.

definition: Extreme poverty or scarcity.
examples: He spent his final years in penury.

definition: Deceitful and untrustworthy; treacherous.
examples: The perfidious ally secretly aided the enemy.

definition: Done with minimal effort or care; routine.
examples: He gave a perfunctory nod and walked on.

definition: Having a harmful effect, especially gradually.
examples: The rumor had a pernicious effect on morale.

definition: Having keen insight and discernment.
examples: Her perspicacious analysis anticipated the crash.

definition: Spreading widely throughout.
examples: A pervasive sense of unease filled the office.

definition: Efforts to improve human welfare, usually by giving money.
examples: Her philanthropy funded dozens of new schools.

definition: Calm and unemotional; not easily excited.
examples: The phlegmatic captain stayed calm through the storm.

definition: Make less angry or hostile; appease.
examples: The airline offered vouchers to placate stranded passengers.

definition: Peaceful, calm, and untroubled.
examples: The placid lake mirrored the mountains.

definition: A trite, overused remark presented as fresh.
examples: The speech offered nothing but platitudes about hard work.

definition: Believable; having the appearance of truth.
examples: He gave a plausible excuse for the delay.

definition: An excess; an overabundance.
examples: The menu offered a plethora of choices.

definition: A strong verbal or written attack on an idea.
examples: The essay was a fierce polemic against censorship.

definition: Heavy and unwieldy; dull and labored.
examples: The ponderous report put readers to sleep.

definition: Practical; concerned with facts and results.
examples: She took a pragmatic approach to the budget crisis.

definition: Dangerously insecure or unstable; likely to fail.
examples: The company's finances were in a precarious state.

definition: Cause to happen suddenly or prematurely.
examples: The scandal precipitated the CEO's resignation.

definition: A preference or special liking for something.
examples: His predilection for risk worried the investors.

definition: Overstepping proper bounds; too bold or forward.
examples: It was presumptuous of him to answer for the whole group.

definition: Attempting to impress by affecting greater importance than is real.
examples: The review mocked the film's pretentious dialogue.

definition: Speak or act evasively to avoid the truth.
examples: Instead of admitting fault, she prevaricated.

definition: In an original, pure, and unspoiled state.
examples: The car was in pristine condition despite its age.

definition: Honesty and strong moral integrity.
examples: The judge was known for her probity and fairness.

definition: Recklessly wasteful; extravagant.
examples: His prodigal spending soon emptied the trust.

definition: Remarkably large or impressive.
examples: She had a prodigious memory for names.

definition: Shamelessly immoral or extravagantly wasteful.
examples: Critics attacked the profligate use of public funds.

definition: Very deep or insightful; far-reaching.
examples: The loss had a profound effect on the whole town.

definition: Increase or spread rapidly.
examples: Copycat apps proliferated after the hit's release.

definition: Tediously long and wordy.
examples: The prolix report could have been half its length.

definition: A natural inclination or tendency.
examples: He has a propensity to interrupt.

definition: Appease or win the favor of someone who is angry.
examples: Offerings were made to propitiate the gods.

definition: Conformity to accepted standards of behavior.
examples: She questioned the propriety of accepting the gift.

definition: Dull and ordinary; lacking imagination.
examples: The explanation was disappointingly prosaic.

definition: Forbid or prohibit; denounce.
examples: The regime proscribed all opposition parties.

definition: Wise and careful in practical matters.
examples: It was prudent to save for emergencies.

definition: Childish and immature.
examples: His puerile pranks embarrassed the whole team.

definition: Eager to argue or fight; combative.
examples: The pugnacious columnist relished a good feud.

definition: Having a sharp taste or smell; biting.
examples: The pungent aroma of garlic filled the kitchen.

definition: A state of uncertainty or perplexity; a dilemma.
examples: She was in a quandary over which offer to accept.

definition: Given to complaining; peevish.
examples: His querulous tone wore on everyone's nerves.

definition: In a state of inactivity or dormancy.
examples: The volcano had been quiescent for a century.

definition: Extremely idealistic and impractical.
examples: His quixotic plan to end all poverty won few backers.

definition: Elevated or exclusive; select and refined.
examples: She moved in the rarefied world of high finance.

definition: Stubbornly resistant to authority or control.
examples: The recalcitrant students ignored every rule.

definition: Withdraw or formally disavow a former statement.
examples: Under pressure, the scientist recanted his findings.

definition: A person who lives in seclusion.
examples: The aging author became a recluse.

definition: Dealing with an obscure subject; hard to understand.
examples: The paper's recondite arguments lost most readers.

definition: Prove to be false or wrong.
examples: The data refuted his central claim.

definition: Assign to an inferior position or place.
examples: The story was relegated to the back page.

definition: Express disapproval or disappointment in.
examples: She reproached him for forgetting the meeting.

definition: Reject or disown; deny the authority of.
examples: The party repudiated its former leader.

definition: Annul or repeal; cancel officially.
examples: The board voted to rescind the controversial rule.

definition: Reserved; reluctant to reveal thoughts or feelings.
examples: She was reticent about her plans for the future.

definition: Feeling or showing deep respect and awe.
examples: A reverent hush fell over the cathedral.

definition: Wise; showing keen judgment and foresight.
examples: The sagacious advisor foresaw the coming crisis.

definition: Most noticeable or important; standing out.
examples: She summarized the salient points in a minute.

definition: Optimistic and hopeful, especially in a hard situation.
examples: She remained sanguine about the company's prospects.

definition: Grimly mocking or cynical.
examples: He gave a sardonic laugh at the naive proposal.

definition: Bitterly harsh or severely critical.
examples: The review was scathing, sparing no one.

definition: Diligent, persistent, and hardworking.
examples: Her sedulous research filled a dozen notebooks.

definition: One inclined to doubt accepted beliefs.
examples: A lifelong skeptic, he questioned every headline.

definition: Showing anxious care or concern for someone.
examples: The nurse was solicitous of every patient's comfort.

definition: Tending to induce drowsiness or sleep.
examples: The lecture's soporific drone put half the room to sleep.

definition: Marked by strict self-discipline and simplicity; austere.
examples: The recruits endured spartan living conditions.

definition: Seemingly true but actually false; deceptively attractive.
examples: His argument was specious but momentarily convincing.

definition: Occurring at irregular intervals; scattered.
examples: Sporadic gunfire could be heard through the night.

definition: Filthy and neglected; morally degraded.
examples: Families were crowded into squalid housing.

definition: Enduring pain or hardship without showing feelings.
examples: He remained stoic even as the news worsened.

definition: Calm and showing little emotion; not easily moved.
examples: The stolid guard ignored the taunts entirely.

definition: Block, hinder, or thwart.
examples: Budget cuts stymied the research team.

definition: Of outstanding grandeur; inspiring awe.
examples: The summit offered a view of sublime beauty.

definition: Support with evidence or proof.
examples: He could not substantiate the accusation.

definition: Replace or take the place of something outdated.
examples: The new rules supersede all previous versions.

definition: Take the place of, especially through scheming.
examples: The deputy quietly supplanted his rival.

definition: An excessive amount; overindulgence.
examples: A surfeit of choices left shoppers paralyzed.

definition: A servile flatterer who fawns to gain advantage.
examples: The king was surrounded by sycophants.

definition: Reserved and saying little.
examples: The taciturn farmer answered only in nods.

definition: Only slightly relevant; going off topic.
examples: His remarks were tangential to the main issue.

definition: Very weak or slight; flimsy.
examples: The link between the two events is tenuous.

definition: Brief and to the point, sometimes curtly so.
examples: Her terse reply ended the conversation.

definition: Timid and fearful.
examples: The timorous recruit flinched at every command.

definition: A long, angry speech of criticism or accusation.
examples: He launched into a tirade against the referee.

definition: Sluggishness, lethargy, or a state of inactivity.
examples: A midsummer torpor settled over the town.

definition: Full of twists and turns; complex or devious.
examples: The negotiations followed a tortuous path.

definition: Easy to control, manage, or shape; docile.
examples: The problem proved more tractable than expected.

definition: Lasting only a short time; temporary.
examples: Fame proved transitory and quickly faded.

definition: Vigorous, incisive, and effective; sharply critical.
examples: Her trenchant critique exposed the plan's flaws.

definition: Overused and lacking freshness; clichéd.
examples: The ending felt trite and predictable.

definition: Aggressively defiant; eager to fight.
examples: He responded to criticism with truculent hostility.

definition: Swollen; or, of language, pompous and overblown.
examples: The turgid prose obscured a simple idea.

definition: Depravity; baseness of character.
examples: He was expelled for moral turpitude.

definition: Present or found everywhere.
examples: Smartphones have become ubiquitous in daily life.

definition: Offense or annoyance.
examples: She took umbrage at the implication that she was lazy.

definition: Leaving no doubt; clear and absolute.
examples: The evidence was unequivocal proof of fraud.

definition: Waver between opinions or actions; be indecisive.
examples: She vacillated for weeks before accepting the offer.

definition: Regard with great respect; revere.
examples: The villagers venerate their elders.

definition: Truthfulness; accuracy.
examples: The court questioned the veracity of his account.

definition: Using more words than needed; wordy.
examples: The manual was so verbose that no one read it.

definition: A trace or remnant of something that once existed.
examples: Not a vestige of the old fort remains.

definition: Capable of working or surviving; practical.
examples: The committee sought a financially viable plan.

definition: Extremely harmful or infectious; bitterly hostile.
examples: The virulent strain spread through the ward.

definition: Cruelly bitter criticism or hostility.
examples: The comments section was full of vitriol.

definition: Loud and insistent in expression; clamorous.
examples: Vociferous protesters filled the square.

definition: Liable to sudden change; unstable or explosive.
examples: The stock proved too volatile for cautious investors.

definition: Speaking easily and at great length; fluent.
examples: She grew voluble after a cup of coffee.

definition: Cautious and watchful against danger.
examples: Investors were wary of the untested market.

definition: Playfully fanciful; erratic and unpredictable.
examples: The garden was full of whimsical sculptures.

definition: Crafty and cunning; full of tricks.
examples: The wily fox slipped past the hounds.

definition: Charming and engaging in a sweet, innocent way.
examples: Her winsome smile put the children at ease.

definition: Great energy or enthusiasm for a cause.
examples: He pursued the reform with tireless zeal.

definition: The highest point; the peak.
examples: The band was at the zenith of its fame.
